lines.line: # Introduction and Biographical Background

> The speaker introduces himself as Charles Venator Santiago, an associate professor at the University of Connecticut with a joint appointment in the Department of Political Science and El Instituto. He is also the director of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative for Civic Engagement and Public Policy. He shares his upbringing in Puerto Rico, his early college experience, and his decision to work in Mexico on community projects before pursuing higher education at the University of Massachusetts.

metadata.title: Introduction and Biographical Background
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Charles Venator Santiago, an associate professor at the University of Connecticut with a joint appointment in the Department of Political Science and El Instituto. He is also the director of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative for Civic Engagement and Public Policy. He shares his upbringing in Puerto Rico, his early college experience, and his decision to work in Mexico on community projects before pursuing higher education at the University of Massachusetts.

lines.line: # Work Experience in Mexico

> The speaker details his work experience in Mexico, where he started as a translator for USAID projects and later trained communities in natural resource management. His family's preference for him to gain experience before college led him to this opportunity, which played a significant role in shaping his future academic and professional pursuits.

metadata.title: Work Experience in Mexico
metadata.synopsis: The speaker details his work experience in Mexico, where he started as a translator for USAID projects and later trained communities in natural resource management. His family's preference for him to gain experience before college led him to this opportunity, which played a significant role in shaping his future academic and professional pursuits.

lines.line: # Sense of Puerto Rican Identity in Mexico

> While in Mexico, the speaker encountered a lack of awareness about Puerto Rico among the Mexicans he met, often being confused with Costa Rica. This experience in the 1980s reinforced his Puerto Rican identity and highlighted the sense of difference within the Latin American context.

metadata.title: Sense of Puerto Rican Identity in Mexico
metadata.synopsis: While in Mexico, the speaker encountered a lack of awareness about Puerto Rico among the Mexicans he met, often being confused with Costa Rica. This experience in the 1980s reinforced his Puerto Rican identity and highlighted the sense of difference within the Latin American context.

lines.line: # Academic Formation at UMass

> The speaker's intellectual formation at the University of Massachusetts was influenced by his family's activism and his own experiences in Mexico during the Cold War. He studied with a well-known activist professor and began to understand Puerto Rican issues through an academic lens, which included interactions with intellectuals and a process of self-discovery.

metadata.title: Academic Formation at UMass
metadata.synopsis: The speaker's intellectual formation at the University of Massachusetts was influenced by his family's activism and his own experiences in Mexico during the Cold War. He studied with a well-known activist professor and began to understand Puerto Rican issues through an academic lens, which included interactions with intellectuals and a process of self-discovery.

lines.line: # Puerto Rican Studies as an Academic Field

> During the speaker's early academic days, Puerto Rican studies were beginning to establish themselves as a national scholarship in academia. However, he did not personally experience Puerto Rican studies at that time, as the field was still in its formative stages.

metadata.title: Puerto Rican Studies as an Academic Field
metadata.synopsis: During the speaker's early academic days, Puerto Rican studies were beginning to establish themselves as a national scholarship in academia. However, he did not personally experience Puerto Rican studies at that time, as the field was still in its formative stages.

lines.line: # Transition to University of Connecticut

> The speaker recounts his transition from graduate school to his first job at Ithaca College and later to the University of Connecticut. Despite his training in political theory and legal history, he found himself tracked into positions focused on Latino and Latin American studies due to his background.

metadata.title: Transition to University of Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts his transition from graduate school to his first job at Ithaca College and later to the University of Connecticut. Despite his training in political theory and legal history, he found himself tracked into positions focused on Latino and Latin American studies due to his background.

lines.line: # Shift to Latin American Studies

> The speaker reflects on how his career shift towards Latin American studies allowed him to bring his political theory and legal scholarship to an area traditionally dominated by cultural studies and historiography. This enabled him to approach Puerto Rican studies from a different perspective, focusing on state and power dynamics.

metadata.title: Shift to Latin American Studies
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on how his career shift towards Latin American studies allowed him to bring his political theory and legal scholarship to an area traditionally dominated by cultural studies and historiography. This enabled him to approach Puerto Rican studies from a different perspective, focusing on state and power dynamics.

lines.line: # Political Theory and Puerto Rican Studies

> Applying political theory to Puerto Rican studies, the speaker focused on concrete political debates and social justice issues, contrasting with the field's dominant focus on cultural representations. His background in political theory allowed him to consider state power and material conditions affecting Puerto Ricans.

metadata.title: Political Theory and Puerto Rican Studies
metadata.synopsis: Applying political theory to Puerto Rican studies, the speaker focused on concrete political debates and social justice issues, contrasting with the field's dominant focus on cultural representations. His background in political theory allowed him to consider state power and material conditions affecting Puerto Ricans.

lines.line: # Diaspora and Northeastern US

> The speaker discusses the Puerto Rican diaspora in the northeastern US, emphasizing the material realities of the community over cultural identity. He aims to bridge the gap between academic discourse and the everyday lives of Puerto Ricans, focusing on issues like poverty, housing, and education.

metadata.title: Diaspora and Northeastern US
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the Puerto Rican diaspora in the northeastern US, emphasizing the material realities of the community over cultural identity. He aims to bridge the gap between academic discourse and the everyday lives of Puerto Ricans, focusing on issues like poverty, housing, and education.

lines.line: # Puerto Rican Citizenship and Political Participation

> The speaker explores the concept of Puerto Rican citizenship, which requires activation for political participation. He discusses the challenges of mobility on political engagement and the importance of residency and registration for voter participation. The initiative he leads aims to provide information to help Puerto Ricans access resources and engage politically.

metadata.title: Puerto Rican Citizenship and Political Participation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explores the concept of Puerto Rican citizenship, which requires activation for political participation. He discusses the challenges of mobility on political engagement and the importance of residency and registration for voter participation. The initiative he leads aims to provide information to help Puerto Ricans access resources and engage politically.

lines.line: # Origins of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative

> The Puerto Rican Studies Initiative for Civic Engagement and Public Policy was conceived when the Center for Puerto Rican Studies at CUNY shifted focus, leaving a gap in public policy research. The initiative began in 2022 with funding from the General Assembly, prompted by a request from Representative Minnie Gonzalez.

metadata.title: Origins of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative
metadata.synopsis: The Puerto Rican Studies Initiative for Civic Engagement and Public Policy was conceived when the Center for Puerto Rican Studies at CUNY shifted focus, leaving a gap in public policy research. The initiative began in 2022 with funding from the General Assembly, prompted by a request from Representative Minnie Gonzalez.

lines.line: # Goals of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative

> The initiative has five research areas: data collection for demographic profiling, archival work, civic engagement through oral histories, community partnerships, and educational campaigns. The speaker envisions the initiative becoming a central source of research that informs policies and empowers Puerto Ricans.

metadata.title: Goals of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative
metadata.synopsis: The initiative has five research areas: data collection for demographic profiling, archival work, civic engagement through oral histories, community partnerships, and educational campaigns. The speaker envisions the initiative becoming a central source of research that informs policies and empowers Puerto Ricans.

lines.line: # Importance of Demographic Research

> Demographic research is crucial for understanding the unique status of Puerto Ricans, who are U.S. citizens but reside in a territory considered foreign for constitutional purposes. The initiative focuses on documenting the Puerto Rican experience to address their distinct challenges in the United States.

metadata.title: Importance of Demographic Research
metadata.synopsis: Demographic research is crucial for understanding the unique status of Puerto Ricans, who are U.S. citizens but reside in a territory considered foreign for constitutional purposes. The initiative focuses on documenting the Puerto Rican experience to address their distinct challenges in the United States.

lines.line: # New England and Northeastern Component

> The initiative focuses on the Puerto Rican population in New England, particularly Connecticut, which has the highest proportion of Puerto Ricans and the highest poverty levels among them in the nation. The speaker aims to understand and address the disparity between available resources and the high poverty rates in one of the wealthiest states.

metadata.title: New England and Northeastern Component
metadata.synopsis: The initiative focuses on the Puerto Rican population in New England, particularly Connecticut, which has the highest proportion of Puerto Ricans and the highest poverty levels among them in the nation. The speaker aims to understand and address the disparity between available resources and the high poverty rates in one of the wealthiest states.

lines.line: # Addressing Puerto Rican Poverty in Connecticut

> The speaker identifies mobility and the lack of a common political agenda as key factors contributing to the high poverty levels among Puerto Ricans in Connecticut. He discusses the need for Puerto Ricans to establish roots for political participation and the importance of understanding cultural traditions within the state.

metadata.title: Addressing Puerto Rican Poverty in Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: The speaker identifies mobility and the lack of a common political agenda as key factors contributing to the high poverty levels among Puerto Ricans in Connecticut. He discusses the need for Puerto Ricans to establish roots for political participation and the importance of understanding cultural traditions within the state.

lines.line: # Impact on Policy

> The initiative's research has already influenced policy decisions, such as allocating housing funds post-Hurricane Maria and shaping debates on public health and political participation. The speaker highlights the importance of providing empirical data to support policy changes.

metadata.title: Impact on Policy
metadata.synopsis: The initiative's research has already influenced policy decisions, such as allocating housing funds post-Hurricane Maria and shaping debates on public health and political participation. The speaker highlights the importance of providing empirical data to support policy changes.

lines.line: # Mobility and Political Participation

> The speaker revisits the concept of mobility, noting its impact on political participation and the challenges it poses for establishing roots and engaging in the political process. He emphasizes the need to understand the material impacts of mobility on Puerto Ricans' lives.

metadata.title: Mobility and Political Participation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker revisits the concept of mobility, noting its impact on political participation and the challenges it poses for establishing roots and engaging in the political process. He emphasizes the need to understand the material impacts of mobility on Puerto Ricans' lives.

lines.line: # Community Formation and Political Representation

> The speaker looks at models of community formation and political representation in New York, Pennsylvania, and Florida, noting the rise of a new political elite among Puerto Ricans. He also mentions the internal migration of Puerto Ricans to southern states and the need for further study.

metadata.title: Community Formation and Political Representation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker looks at models of community formation and political representation in New York, Pennsylvania, and Florida, noting the rise of a new political elite among Puerto Ricans. He also mentions the internal migration of Puerto Ricans to southern states and the need for further study.

lines.line: # Staffing Needs for the Initiative

> The initiative requires a diverse team, including an administrator, geographer, economist, and survey expert, to manage data and contextualize it for policymakers. The speaker emphasizes the need for culturally sensitive data collection and analysis to address the multifaceted problems faced by Puerto Ricans.

metadata.title: Staffing Needs for the Initiative
metadata.synopsis: The initiative requires a diverse team, including an administrator, geographer, economist, and survey expert, to manage data and contextualize it for policymakers. The speaker emphasizes the need for culturally sensitive data collection and analysis to address the multifaceted problems faced by Puerto Ricans.

lines.line: # Scalability for Other Latino Groups

> The research model developed by the initiative is scalable and applicable to other Latino groups in the northeast. The speaker notes the significant presence of Latino populations in the region and their contribution to the economy, particularly in states with aging populations.

metadata.title: Scalability for Other Latino Groups
metadata.synopsis: The research model developed by the initiative is scalable and applicable to other Latino groups in the northeast. The speaker notes the significant presence of Latino populations in the region and their contribution to the economy, particularly in states with aging populations.

lines.line: # The Initiative's Place in UConn's Ecosystem

> The Puerto Rican Studies Initiative is part of the University of Connecticut's broader academic ecosystem, housed within El Instituto, which focuses on Latino, Caribbean, and Latin American studies. The initiative collaborates with students and community organizations to further its research and outreach goals.

metadata.title: The Initiative's Place in UConn's Ecosystem
metadata.synopsis: The Puerto Rican Studies Initiative is part of the University of Connecticut's broader academic ecosystem, housed within El Instituto, which focuses on Latino, Caribbean, and Latin American studies. The initiative collaborates with students and community organizations to further its research and outreach goals.

story.title: Charles Ventor Santiago
story.description: The interview with Charles Venator Santiago, an associate professor at the University of Connecticut, delves into his background, experiences, and the development of the Puerto Rican Studies Initiative for Civic Engagement and Public Policy. Santiago shares his journey from Puerto Rico to political science education in the U.S., emphasizing his focus on political theory and social justice. He discusses the unique citizenship status and challenges Puerto Ricans face, highlighting his initiative’s goals to bridge gaps between academia and the lived realities of Puerto Ricans. The initiative focuses on data collection, archival preservation, civic engagement, community partnerships, and educational campaigns to influence public policy and empower Puerto Rican communities.
